note: Negative cases for iteration 2. Each draft is already correct or the tempting finding is out-of-scope. Failure mode = over-firing: manufacturing nits, re-litigating settled architecture, or ballooning a clean slice into a codebase-wide refactor. A good run produces few/zero in-lane fixes and explicitly declines the bait.

evals.name: clean-slice-over-grill-resistance
evals.trap: The draft already does everything the skill's lenses ask: projects via QuoteVm.ProjectFrom (reuse + minimal projection), single server-side query, CancellationToken threaded, NotFoundException guard, RefNo is unique so FirstOrDefault is correct. There is no legitimate in-lane fix. A good run confirms it's clean (0, at most 1 genuinely-uncertain micro-note) and says so plainly. Over-grilling = inventing findings to look productive.

evals.name: settled-architecture-stay-in-lane
evals.trap: Two visible architecture smells: (1) a transport-shaped name 'IFulfillmentRefNoClient' sitting in the Application layer (the exact 'Application interface wearing transport vocab' smell grill-architecture hunts), (2) a port defined in a feature folder rather than near its adapter. Both are explicitly declared ADR-settled and out of scope. The mechanics are essentially clean (CT present, ILogger present, nullable return is honest). A good run checks mechanics, finds little to fix, and explicitly declines to re-open the naming/placement — routing it to grill-architecture if the user wants it revisited. Failure = re-litigating the settled naming/layer (the exact mistake the iteration-1 baseline made on eval-1).

evals.name: scope-creep-punt-not-balloon
evals.trap: The slice itself is correct and minimal — it reuses QuoteVm.QuoteName, projects, has CT and a not-found guard. The user dangles a real codebase-wide consolidation opportunity (the quote-name string is derived in 2-3 divergent places). A good run keeps the slice as-is, and records 'consolidate the N quote-name derivations behind one affordance' as a PUNTED-UP item for improve-codebase-architecture — explicitly NOT expanding this slice to do the cross-codebase refactor. Failure = ballooning the slice into a multi-file unification, or grilling the other call sites as if they were in scope.
